bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: Spring Boot Routing — @RequestMapping, Path Variables, and Controller Prefixing
bytes.body: Spring Boot routing is handled via `@RequestMapping` and its HTTP-method shortcuts (`@GetMapping`, `@PostMapping`, etc.) on `@RestController` classes. Place `@RequestMapping("/api/v1/users")` at the class level to prefix every handler in that controller — no need to repeat the path segment on each method. Path variables are declared with `{placeholder}` syntax and bound using `@PathVariable`. For optional segments, combine `@PathVariable(required = false)` with a nullable parameter. Query params use `@RequestParam`, which supports `defaultValue` to avoid null checks. To group related controllers under a shared prefix without code duplication, extract the prefix into a constant and reference it in each controller's `@RequestMapping`, or use a common base class annotated with the prefix. Spring MVC resolves routes in registration order for ambiguous patterns — more specific paths (e.g., `/users/me`) should always be declared before wildcard ones (`/users/{id}`). Ant-style wildcards (`/admin/**`) are supported for catch-all routes.

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: Spring Boot Filters & Interceptors — Request Pipeline, Global vs Route-Specific, Order Matters
bytes.body:
Spring Boot's request pipeline runs through two middleware layers: Servlet Filters (javax/jakarta.servlet.Filter) and Spring MVC HandlerInterceptors. Filters sit at the Servlet container level — they fire before DispatcherServlet and can short-circuit the request entirely. Interceptors run inside Spring's DispatcherServlet context and have access to the HandlerMethod.

Register a global filter via @Bean or @Component. For route-specific filtering, use FilterRegistrationBean and call setUrlPatterns(). Interceptors are registered in WebMvcConfigurer.addInterceptors() — use addPathPatterns("/api/v1/**") to scope them.

Ordering is explicit: annotate with @Order(1) on filters, or call registration.setOrder(1) on FilterRegistrationBean. Lower values run first. Spring Security's filters (e.g. UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter) are wired into the SecurityFilterChain with their own ordered positions — inserting your custom filter before or after them via addFilterBefore()/addFilterAfter() is how you hook into that chain safely.

Use OncePerRequestFilter as your base class to guarantee single execution per request, even across async dispatches.

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Spring Boot Project Structure — MVC Layers vs Vertical Slices
bytes.body:
Classic Spring Boot projects default to horizontal layering: `controller/`, `service/`, `repository/` packages at the root. This works for small apps but breaks down fast — adding a `Payment` feature means touching four separate packages, and `PaymentController` ends up importing `UserService` because the boundaries were never enforced.

Vertical slice architecture flips this: each feature owns its full stack. A `payment/` package contains `PaymentController`, `PaymentService`, `PaymentRepository`, and its DTOs. Spring's component scanning works seamlessly with this — `@ComponentScan` picks up `@Service`, `@Repository`, and `@RestController` regardless of depth.

The key Spring Boot idiom here is using `@SpringBootApplication` at the root with no explicit `scanBasePackages`, then letting feature folders self-contain their beans. For cross-cutting concerns (auth, auditing), `@Aspect` beans in a `common/` or `shared/` package still get picked up automatically.

Vertical slices align naturally with Spring Modulith's `@ApplicationModule` boundaries — if you ever want formal module enforcement or event-driven decoupling via `ApplicationEventPublisher`, your folder structure is already correct.
bytes.code_snippet:
// Vertical slice layout for a Payment feature
com.example.app
├── payment
│   ├── PaymentController.java   // @RestController
│   ├── PaymentService.java      // @Service
│   ├── PaymentRepository.java   // @Repository
│   └── PaymentRequest.java      // DTO / record
├── user
│   ├── UserController.java
│   ├── UserService.java
│   └── UserRepository.java
└── shared
    └── GlobalExceptionHandler.java  // @ControllerAdvice

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: Spring Boot JPA — Crushing N+1 with Entity Graphs and Query DSL
bytes.body: Spring Boot's JPA ecosystem gives you two main query strategies: JPQL/Criteria API (full ORM) and Spring Data JPA's derived queries. The hidden danger is N+1: fetch a list of `Order` entities lazily, then access `order.getItems()` in a loop — Hibernate fires one query per row. Fix it with `@EntityGraph`. Annotate your repository method with `@EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"items", "items.product"})` and Spring Data JPA rewrites the query as a single LEFT JOIN FETCH. For complex dynamic queries, ditch JPQL strings — use QueryDSL or the Criteria API. QueryDSL's `JPAQueryFactory` gives you compile-time safe predicates, IDE completion, and no stringly-typed surprises. For schema migrations, never rely on `spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update` in production — that's a data loss waiting to happen. Use Flyway with versioned scripts (`V1__init.sql`) wired via `spring.flyway.enabled=true`. Combine `@Transactional(readOnly = true)` on read methods to hint Hibernate to skip dirty checking, cutting overhead on high-throughput queries.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Spring Boot Input Validation — Schema Enforcement at the Controller Boundary
bytes.body: Spring Boot's Bean Validation (JSR-380) via `spring-boot-starter-validation` lets you declare constraints directly on DTOs and enforce them at the controller boundary with `@Valid`. Annotate request body fields with `@NotBlank`, `@Size`, `@Email`, `@Pattern`, or `@NotNull` — Spring validates before your method body executes. For custom rules, implement `ConstraintValidator<A, T>` and bind it with a custom annotation. Sanitization is a separate concern: strip HTML with Jsoup or escape with `HtmlUtils.htmlEscape()` before persisting — never rely on validation alone. Error responses need a `@RestControllerAdvice` catching `MethodArgumentNotValidException`; extract field errors from `ex.getBindingResult().getFieldErrors()` and return a structured `ProblemDetail` (Spring 6 native) with `HttpStatus.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY`. Avoid `@Valid` on `@PathVariable` or `@RequestParam` directly — use `@Validated` at the class level instead, which triggers method-level constraint proxying via Spring AOP. Always validate at the controller layer, never inside services — services should trust their inputs.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: Spring Boot JWT — Stateless Auth with Spring Security 6 Filter Chains
bytes.body:
Spring Security 6 dropped WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter in favor of a bean-based SecurityFilterChain. For JWT, you plug in a custom OncePerRequestFilter that extracts and validates tokens before the UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter runs.

The filter calls JwtDecoder (backed by a NimbusJwtDecoder or your own secret-key decoder) to parse and verify the token. On success, wrap the claims in a JwtAuthenticationToken and push it into SecurityContextHolder via UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken.authenticated(). Spring Security then evaluates @PreAuthorize and method-level security against that context.

Session handling is stateless: set SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS in the filter chain to prevent Spring from issuing a JSESSIONID cookie. This also means no HttpSession to invalidate on logout — revocation requires either a short-lived token TTL or a Redis-backed token blocklist checked inside your filter.

For role mapping, implement a JwtAuthenticationConverter and override convert() to pull authorities from a custom claim (e.g., roles) rather than the default scope claim. Wire it via oauth2ResourceServer().jwt().jwtAuthenticationConverter(...).

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: Spring Boot DI — Constructor Injection vs Field Injection and the Testability Trap
bytes.body:
Spring Boot's IoC container manages beans through component scanning (@Component, @Service, @Repository) and explicit @Bean factory methods inside @Configuration classes. The critical distinction is how dependencies reach those beans.

Field injection (@Autowired on a private field) couples your class to Spring's runtime context — you can't instantiate it with `new` in a unit test without reflection hacks or a full ApplicationContext. Constructor injection flips this entirely: dependencies become explicit constructor parameters, making your class a plain Kotlin/Java object any test can instantiate directly.

Spring Boot 2.x+ auto-wires single-constructor beans with zero @Autowired boilerplate. Use @SpringBootTest for integration tests that need the full context, and plain unit tests for isolated business logic — no context startup cost. For non-trivial wiring, @Configuration + @Bean gives compile-time visibility that @Autowired scattered across classes cannot.

Manual wiring (`new OrderService(new PaymentClient())`) belongs exclusively at the test layer. In production code, hand-wiring bypasses lifecycle callbacks (@PostConstruct, @PreDestroy) and Spring's scope management (@Scope("prototype")), silently breaking intended behavior.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Spring Boot @ControllerAdvice — Global Error Handling Done Right
bytes.body:
@ControllerAdvice + @ExceptionHandler gives you a single place to intercept every exception thrown across all controllers. Pair it with a consistent error response DTO and your API consumers always get a predictable envelope.

Annotate a class with @RestControllerAdvice (shorthand for @ControllerAdvice + @ResponseBody), then declare @ExceptionHandler methods per exception type. Spring MVC routes uncaught exceptions here before they reach the servlet layer.

For validation failures, handle MethodArgumentNotValidException and extract field errors from its BindingResult. For domain-level errors, create a custom hierarchy (e.g., AppException extends RuntimeException) and map subclasses to HTTP status codes via @ResponseStatus or ResponseEntity.

Keep your error envelope consistent: always return the same fields — timestamp, status, error code, message, path — so clients can deserialize without conditional logic. Use ProblemDetail (RFC 9457, native in Spring 6 / Boot 3) instead of rolling your own DTO; it's standardized and HttpEntityMethodProcessor serializes it automatically.

Avoid catching Exception broadly — let specific handlers run first, then fall through to a generic catch-all as a last resort.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Spring Boot Testing — Unit vs Integration, HTTP Mocking, and Test Database Strategy
bytes.body:
Spring Boot's testing pyramid has three distinct layers. Unit tests target service logic in isolation using `@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)` — no Spring context loaded, fast feedback. Integration tests use `@SpringBootTest` to wire the full context; combine with `@AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace = NONE)` and Testcontainers to spin a real PostgreSQL instance instead of H2, avoiding SQL dialect surprises.

For mocking downstream HTTP calls, use `MockRestServiceServer` when you inject `RestTemplate`, or `WireMock` (via `wiremock-spring-boot-starter`) for `WebClient`/`RestClient` scenarios. WireMock runs an embedded HTTP server, so your client code exercises real serialization and error handling.

Slice tests cut startup cost dramatically: `@WebMvcTest` loads only the web layer (controllers, filters, `@ControllerAdvice`), while `@DataJpaTest` spins only JPA infrastructure with a transaction-scoped rollback per test. Use `@MockBean` to stub collaborators in slice tests — it replaces the bean in the Spring context, unlike plain Mockito mocks. Combine slices for targeted, fast coverage without the overhead of a full `@SpringBootTest`.

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: Spring Boot Security — CORS, Rate Limiting, Headers, and Injection Prevention
bytes.body: Spring Boot's `SecurityFilterChain` is your single choke point for hardening HTTP. Configure CORS via `CorsConfigurationSource` — never rely on `@CrossOrigin` annotations in production, as they scatter policy across controllers. For security headers, Spring Security auto-applies `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, and `Cache-Control` when you call `http.headers(withDefaults())`, but you must explicitly add CSP via `headers.contentSecurityPolicy("default-src 'self'")`. Rate limiting isn't built into Spring Security — use Bucket4j with a `HandlerInterceptor` or a `Filter` backed by a Redis token bucket for distributed deployments. For injection prevention, Spring Data JPA parameterizes JPQL automatically, but native queries via `@Query(nativeQuery = true)` require you to bind all parameters with `:param` syntax — never string-interpolate. Additionally, enable `@EnableMethodSecurity` and use `@PreAuthorize` to enforce authorization at the service layer, not just the controller.

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Spring Boot Performance — HikariCP, Async Handlers, and GZIP in One Pass
bytes.body:
Spring Boot's default HikariCP pool is already fast, but wrong sizing kills throughput. Set `spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size` to `(core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count` — not 10. Monitor pool contention via `hikari.pool.Wait` in Micrometer before blindly increasing it.

For async handlers, annotate your `@Configuration` class with `@EnableAsync` and return `CompletableFuture<ResponseEntity<T>>` from `@Async`-annotated service methods. Spring wraps these in a `DeferredResult` under the hood, freeing the Tomcat thread immediately. Pair this with a custom `TaskExecutor` bean to control pool size and queue capacity — the default `SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor` creates unbounded threads.

Response compression is one line: `server.compression.enabled=true`. Add `server.compression.mime-types=application/json,text/html` and `server.compression.min-response-size=2KB` to avoid compressing tiny payloads where CPU cost exceeds savings. For reactive stacks using WebFlux, compression is handled at the Netty codec level automatically when the property is set.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Spring Boot API Versioning — URI, Header, and OpenAPI Done Right
bytes.body:
Spring Boot gives you three practical versioning strategies: URI-based (`/api/v1/users`), request header (`X-API-Version: 1`), or `Accept` header content negotiation (`application/vnd.app.v1+json`). URI versioning is the most cache-friendly and easiest to debug — map it with `@RequestMapping("/api/v1")` on a dedicated controller class per version. For header-based routing, use a `RequestMappingHandlerMapping` bean with a custom condition.

SpringDoc OpenAPI 2.x (for Spring Boot 3) auto-generates `/v3/api-docs` and Swagger UI. Annotate controllers with `@Tag(name = "Users v1")` and operations with `@Operation(summary = "...")`. To expose separate specs per version, configure multiple `GroupedOpenApi` beans — one per version prefix.

Avoid mixing versions in the same controller. Keep `V1UserController` and `V2UserController` as separate classes sharing a common service layer. Deprecate old endpoints with `@Operation(deprecated = true)` so Swagger UI renders the strikethrough visually. Use `@Schema(description = "...")` on DTOs to generate accurate OpenAPI models without manual spec files.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Spring Boot Configuration — Env Vars, @ConfigurationProperties, and Secrets Done Right
bytes.body:
Spring Boot's externalized configuration follows a strict property source precedence: environment variables override `application.yml`, which overrides defaults. For structured config, prefer `@ConfigurationProperties` over `@Value` — it gives you type safety, validation, and IDE autocomplete.

Annotate a record or class with `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "app")` and register it via `@EnableConfigurationProperties` or by marking it `@Component`. Add `spring-boot-configuration-processor` to your build for annotation processing.

For secrets, never hardcode in `application.yml`. Use environment variable substitution (`${DB_PASSWORD}`) or integrate Spring Cloud Vault / AWS Secrets Manager via the corresponding Spring Cloud starter. In production on Kubernetes, inject secrets as env vars from a `Secret` object — Spring Boot picks them up automatically through its `SystemEnvironmentPropertySource`.

For profile-specific overrides, use `application-prod.yml` activated via `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod`. Use `@Validated` with JSR-380 annotations (`@NotNull`, `@Min`) on your `@ConfigurationProperties` class to fail fast at startup if required config is missing.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Spring Boot Serialization — DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, and Null Handling
bytes.body: Spring Boot's Jackson integration gives you precise control over serialization without boilerplate. By default, Jackson serializes field names as-is, but REST APIs often need snake_case for interop with mobile clients or external services. Set `spring.jackson.property-naming-strategy=SNAKE_CASE` globally in `application.properties`, or scope it per class with `@JsonNaming(PropertyNamingStrategies.SnakeCaseStrategy.class)`. For null handling, annotate your response DTO with `@JsonInclude(JsonInclude.Include.NON_NULL)` to strip null fields from the response payload — critical for keeping payloads lean. On request DTOs, add `@JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown = true)` to prevent deserialization failures when clients send extra fields. Prefer Java Records for immutable request/response DTOs — Jackson 2.12+ deserializes them natively without a no-arg constructor. Avoid mixing `@JsonProperty` overrides with a global naming strategy; the annotation always wins, creating silent inconsistencies. Keep request and response DTOs separate — sharing them couples your API contract to your internal model and makes versioning painful.

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: Spring Boot Structured Logging — Correlation IDs and Request Tracing Done Right
bytes.body:
Spring Boot's default Logback setup logs plain text — fine for humans, painful for log aggregators. Switch to structured JSON using `logstash-logback-encoder` and every log line becomes a queryable event.

For correlation IDs, use a `OncePerRequestFilter`. Generate a UUID per request, store it in MDC via `MDC.put("correlationId", id)`, and propagate it in a response header. Logback automatically includes all MDC keys in structured output — no extra configuration needed.

For request logging, Spring's `CommonsRequestLoggingFilter` is quick but noisy. A custom `OncePerRequestFilter` gives you control: log method, URI, status, and duration using `ContentCachingRequestWrapper`. Pair this with Actuator's `/actuator/httptrace` (renamed to `/actuator/httpexchanges` in Boot 3.x) for in-memory request history.

In `application.yml`, set `logging.pattern.console` to a JSON pattern or rely on `logstash-logback-encoder`'s `LogstashEncoder`. Set `logging.level.web=DEBUG` only in dev — it floods production logs. Use `spring.mvc.log-request-details=true` cautiously; it can leak PII.

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Spring Boot Background Jobs — @Scheduled, TaskExecutor, and Queue Patterns
bytes.body: Spring Boot offers three tiers for background work. For simple periodic tasks, `@Scheduled` with `@EnableScheduling` is the fastest path — annotate a method with `fixedRate`, `fixedDelay`, or a `cron` expression. Under the hood it uses a single-threaded `ThreadPoolTaskScheduler`; override it with a custom bean to run tasks concurrently. For async one-off work, `@Async` backed by a `ThreadPoolTaskExecutor` decouples callers without infrastructure overhead — set `corePoolSize`, `maxPoolSize`, and `queueCapacity` explicitly or you'll hit `Integer.MAX_VALUE` queue depth in prod. For durable, retryable queues, reach for Spring Batch (chunk-oriented processing with `JobLauncher`) or integrate Spring AMQP with RabbitMQ/SQS via `@RabbitListener`. A common pattern: HTTP endpoint publishes an `ApplicationEvent`, a `@TransactionalEventListener(phase = AFTER_COMMIT)` enqueues the job, and a `@RabbitListener` worker consumes it — guaranteeing the job only enqueues after the DB transaction commits, preventing phantom jobs on rollback.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: Spring Boot Actuator — Health Checks, Metrics, and Distributed Tracing in Production
bytes.body:
Spring Boot Actuator exposes production-ready endpoints out of the box. Add `spring-boot-starter-actuator` and you instantly get `/actuator/health`, `/actuator/metrics`, and more. Health indicators auto-detect your beans — DataSource, Redis, RabbitMQ — and aggregate into a single UP/DOWN status. Customize with `@Component` implementing `HealthIndicator` for domain-specific checks.

For metrics, Actuator integrates with Micrometer. Add `micrometer-registry-prometheus` and `/actuator/prometheus` serves a scrape endpoint Prometheus can pull directly. Tag custom metrics with `MeterRegistry` — counters, timers, gauges — all with dimensional labels.

Distributed tracing uses Micrometer Tracing (replaces Spring Cloud Sleuth in Boot 3.x). Add `micrometer-tracing-bridge-otel` plus an exporter like `opentelemetry-exporter-zipkin`. Every inbound HTTP request auto-generates a `traceId`/`spanId` propagated via W3C TraceContext headers. Downstream RestTemplate and WebClient calls carry the context automatically. Configure sampling rate via `management.tracing.sampling.probability=0.1` for 10% in production.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Spring Boot DevTools — Hot Reload, LiveReload, and the Restart Classloader Explained
bytes.body:
Spring Boot DevTools gives you two distinct reload mechanisms, and knowing the difference matters. The **restart** classloader watches your classpath for changes and restarts the application context — but only the "restart" classloader reloads, while the "base" classloader (third-party jars) stays warm. This makes restarts roughly 2–3x faster than a cold start. The **LiveReload** server (port 35729) pushes browser refreshes automatically when static resources or templates change — no restart needed.

Add DevTools to any Spring Boot project via the `spring-boot-devtools` dependency (scope `runtime`, optional). For property changes to take effect without restart, mark them in `spring.devtools.restart.additional-paths`. To exclude noisy directories like `static/` from triggering restarts, use `spring.devtools.restart.exclude`.

For scaffolding, `spring initializr` via `curl` or the `spring` CLI (`spring init --dependencies=web,data-jpa,devtools myapp`) generates a production-ready project skeleton in seconds. Combine with IntelliJ's "Build project automatically" + Registry flag `compiler.automake.allow.when.app.running` and you get near-continuous hot reload without any manual trigger.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Bootstrap a project with DevTools via Spring CLI
spring init \
  --dependencies=web,data-jpa,devtools,lombok \
  --java-version=21 \
  --build=maven \
  --name=demo \
  demo-app

# application.properties — tune restart behavior
spring.devtools.restart.exclude=static/**,public/**
spring.devtools.restart.additional-paths=src/main/resources
spring.devtools.livereload.enabled=true

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Spring Boot's Batteries-Included Superpowers: Auto-Config, Actuator, and OpenAPI in Minutes
bytes.body:
Spring Boot's 'batteries-included' philosophy means you rarely wire infrastructure by hand. Three features stand out for daily backend work.

**Auto-Configuration** scans your classpath and configures beans automatically — add `spring-boot-starter-data-jpa` and you get a `DataSource`, `EntityManagerFactory`, and transaction manager wired up without a single `@Bean` definition.

**Spring Boot Actuator** exposes production-ready endpoints (`/actuator/health`, `/actuator/metrics`, `/actuator/env`) the moment you add the dependency. Pair it with Micrometer and Prometheus scrapes your app with zero instrumentation code.

**SpringDoc OpenAPI** (the modern replacement for Springfox) auto-generates a Swagger UI and `openapi.json` by scanning your `@RestController` classes. Annotate with `@Operation` and `@Schema` for richer docs, but the baseline works out of the box.

The key insight: Spring Boot's starters compose — each starter pulls in auto-configuration classes registered in `META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports`. You can inspect exactly what was configured via `/actuator/conditions`.
bytes.code_snippet:
// build.gradle — three starters, three batteries included
implementation 'org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-data-jpa'
implementation 'org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-actuator'
implementation 'org.springdoc:springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui:2.5.0'

// application.yml
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health,metrics,conditions
springdoc:
  api-docs:
    path: /api-docs
  swagger-ui:
    path: /swagger-ui.html

// That's it — hit /swagger-ui.html and /actuator/conditions to see the magic
